Title: Strigolactones, a Novel Carotenoid-Derived Plant Hormone

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporter; pleiotropic drug resistance (PDR) efflux pump
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes arbuscular mycorrhizal symbiosis; regulates shoot branching/root responses
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Exports strigolactones to root exudates/rhizosphere; Phpdr1 mutants show reduced SL in root exudates despite similar root SL levels
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; root exudate metabolite measurement; heterologous overexpression in Arabidopsis with GR24 response assay
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PhPDR1 was identified as an SL export carrier required for release of SLs into the soil. 2. Phpdr1 mutants retained near-WT SL levels in roots but had much lower SL levels in root exudates and reduced interaction with Glomus intraradices. 3. Ectopic PhPDR1 overexpression in Arabidopsis counteracted the root growth inhibition caused by high GR24, supporting an export function.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in individual subepidermal cells of lateral roots; also highly expressed near axillary buds
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phosphate deficiency decreases PDR1 expression in petunia
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering rhizosphere SL release to optimize mycorrhization and root–soil signaling; caution because altering exudation may affect parasitic weed interactions
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced SL export could potentially increase germination of parasitic weeds
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper; gene highlighted for transport rather than biosynthesis. Evidence for shootward SL transport role remains unresolved.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Nicotiana benthamiana; Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Initial isomerization step from carotenoid precursor pool
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reversibly interconverts all-trans-β-carotene and 9-cis-β-carotene to supply the stereospecific substrate for CCD7
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for providing correct stereoisomer substrate; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes strigolactone biosynthesis by generating 9-cis-β-carotene required for downstream cleavage; d27 mutants show increased tillering/branching
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro enzyme assay with pure carotene isomers; mutant phenotype analysis; grafting; transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. D27 was identified as the enzyme catalyzing reversible all-trans/9-cis isomerization of β-carotene. 2. This reaction explains the 9-cis substrate preference of CCD7 in SL biosynthesis. 3. d27 mutants show branching/tillering phenotypes consistent with reduced SL formation, though residual 9-cis-β-carotene suggests additional sources may exist.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In Medicago truncatula, phosphate and nitrogen deficiency enhance MtD27 expression; in rice, D27 expression decreases after phosphate resupply and is reduced by OsNSP1/OsNSP2 knockdown
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phosphate deficiency induces D27 expression; phosphate resupply represses it
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CCD7; CCD8; MAX1 homologs in pathway context; OsNSP1; OsNSP2 (transcriptional control)
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering or editing D27 can tune SL levels to modify tillering/branching and nutrient-responsive architecture
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing D27 may impair adaptive phosphate-starvation responses and mycorrhizal signaling while increasing tillering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper. Higher plants contain two D27 homologs with unclear functions; d27 phenotypes are milder than other SL mutants, suggesting partial redundancy or nonenzymatic formation of 9-cis-β-carotene.

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Arabidopsis MAX3; rice D17/HTD1; pea RMS5; petunia DAD3
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Carotenoid cleavage oxygenase; nonheme iron enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Pisum sativum; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; Rice; Pea; Escherichia coli; Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Pisum sativum; Escherichia coli; Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Early oxidative cleavage branch from β-carotene
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Stereospecifically cleaves 9-cis-β-carotene at the C9'-C10' double bond to form 9-cis-β-apo-10'-carotenal and β-ionone
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step in core pathway, but not explicitly designated rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for strigolactone biosynthesis; loss causes branching/tillering phenotypes and reduced SL formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in carotenoid-accumulating E. coli; in vitro assays with pure stereoisomer substrates; mutant analyses; transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CCD7 enzymes from Arabidopsis, pea, and rice specifically cleave 9-cis-β-carotene rather than all-trans isomers. 2. The products are 9-cis-β-apo-10'-carotenal and β-ionone, providing the direct substrate for CCD8. 3. Genetic and biochemical evidence across species place CCD7 as an essential early enzyme in SL biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Arabidopsis CCD7/MAX3 highest in roots; rice CCD7 in vascular bundles throughout the plant; tomato CCD7 highest in immature green fruits
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In Medicago truncatula, MtCCD7 is induced by phosphate and nitrogen deficiency; in rice D17/HTD1 expression decreases after phosphate resupply
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Auxin promotes expression; phosphate deficiency induces expression; GR24/SL feedback represses biosynthetic gene expression
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to modify branching/tillering and nutrient-responsive architecture through SL content manipulation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced CCD7 may compromise phosphate-starvation adaptation and symbiotic signaling; may alter other apocarotenoid pathways such as mycorradicin/cyclohexenone pigment formation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CCD7 can cleave other 9-cis carotenoids in vitro (e.g., 9-cis-lutein, 9-cis-zeaxanthin), suggesting possible additional apocarotenoid roles.

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Arabidopsis MAX4; rice D10; pea RMS1; petunia DAD1
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Carotenoid cleavage oxygenase; nonheme iron enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Pisum sativum; Petunia hybrida
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; Rice; Pea; Escherichia coli; Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Pisum sativum; Escherichia coli; Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream; committed transformation to SL scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 9-cis-β-apo-10'-carotenal into carlactone through repeated oxygenation, intramolecular rearrangement, and butenolide formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step forming central SL scaffold; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for production of carlactone and downstream SLs; mutants show branching/tillering phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro enzyme assays with 9-cis and all-trans substrates; isotope labeling; mutant rescue with carlactone; endogenous metabolite detection; transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CCD8 converts 9-cis-β-apo-10'-carotenal into carlactone, a breakthrough intermediate linking carotenoids to SLs. 2. Carlactone is produced in the biologically relevant 11R configuration and functions as an in planta SL precursor. 3. Carlactone rescues SL-deficient rice tillering and stimulates Striga germination, supporting physiological relevance.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Generally high in roots; Arabidopsis MAX4 high in root tips, styles, stigmata, siliques, low in nodal tissues around buds
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In Medicago truncatula, MtCCD8 is induced by phosphate and nitrogen deficiency; in rice D10 decreases after phosphate resupply
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Auxin promotes expression; phosphate deficiency induces expression; GR24/SL feedback represses expression; ABA deficiency lowers CCD8 transcript levels in some systems
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for regulating SL content, tillering, mycorrhization, and phosphate starvation responses
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may alter drought/salt responses, symbiosis, and possibly other apocarotenoid-derived processes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CCD8 also cleaves all-trans-β-apo-10'-carotenal to β-apo-13-carotenone in vitro, but in planta significance remains unresolved.

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Arabidopsis MAX1; rice Os900/Os1400/Os1900/Os5100 plus one nonfunctional homolog
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Carlactone oxidase / strigolactone-forming cytochrome P450 homologs
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Nicotiana benthamiana; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Arabidopsis/rice MAX1 homologs oxidize and cyclize carlactone to downstream strigolactones; rice Os900 converts carlactone to 4-deoxyorobanchol and Os1400 hydroxylates 4-deoxyorobanchol to orobanchol
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant for conversion of carlactone to ABC-ring-containing SLs; key control point in pathway diversification
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes conversion of carlactone into downstream bioactive SLs; max1 mutants accumulate carlactone and exhibit branching phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant metabolite accumulation; grafting; 13C-carlactone feeding; transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; yeast microsome assays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MAX1 acts downstream of D27/CCD7/CCD8 and converts carlactone into downstream SLs. 2. Rice Os900 was shown to be a carlactone oxidase producing 4-deoxyorobanchol, whereas Os1400 converts 4-deoxyorobanchol to orobanchol. 3. MAX1 homolog diversification likely underlies SL structural diversity, including formation of distinct orobanchol- and strigol-like SLs.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Arabidopsis MAX1 is expressed in vascular-associated tissues and axillary regions of leaves and flowers; in roots only in central cylinder above differentiation zone
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In Medicago truncatula, MtMAX1 is induced by phosphate and nitrogen deficiency and is controlled by NSP1/NSP2
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phosphate and nitrogen deficiency enhance MtMAX1 expression; phosphate resupply reduces expression of upstream pathway genes in rice
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Excellent target for engineering specific SL profiles and reducing or increasing particular SL structures relevant to architecture, mycorrhiza, or parasitic weed interactions
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changing MAX1 activity may shift SL composition with uncertain developmental and rhizosphere consequences
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This review treats MAX1 as a biosynthetic enzyme family. Individual rice homologs have distinct catalytic roles; one homolog is nonfunctional.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytochrome P450 CYP711 family; rice MAX1 homolog
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Nicotiana benthamiana; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes and catalyzes ring-forming conversion of 11R-carlactone into 4-deoxyorobanchol
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key terminal determinant for orobanchol-like parent SL formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly decreases carlactone and forms 4-deoxyorobanchol when coexpressed with upstream genes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient coexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana; yeast microsome enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Os900 was identified as the rice MAX1 homolog that converts 11R-carlactone to 4-deoxyorobanchol. 2. This established the enzymatic route from carlactone to an ABC-ring-containing parent SL. 3. Os900 therefore defines a major biosynthetic branch producing orobanchol-like SLs.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for tailoring rice SL composition, potentially useful for balancing tillering, mycorrhization, and parasitic weed interactions
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing Os900 may alter rhizosphere signaling and nutrient-responsive architecture
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A specific rice MAX1 homolog rather than generic Arabidopsis MAX1 catalytic function.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytochrome P450 CYP711 family; rice MAX1 homolog
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Nicotiana benthamiana; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream; terminal step for orobanchol formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates 4-deoxyorobanchol at C-4 to form orobanchol
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts 4-deoxyorobanchol into orobanchol, contributing to SL structural diversification
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; expression in yeast; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Os1400 was identified as the rice enzyme converting 4-deoxyorobanchol into orobanchol. 2. The result shows that B-C ring modification in the orobanchol-like subfamily occurs after parent SL formation. 3. Os1400 exemplifies how MAX1 homologs diversify SL structures.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for altering the specific SL blend released by roots or present in planta
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changing specific SL composition may affect interactions with fungi and parasitic weeds differently from total SL amount
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Formerly designated ent-2'-epi-5-deoxystrigol-4-hydroxylase in the review.

Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: F-box protein in SCF ubiquitin ligase complex
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Strigolactone signal transduction and downstream developmental/stress responses
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation of signaling repressors/targets after SL perception
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: D53-like repressors/proteins targeted in SCF complex; signaling outputs affecting PIN1 and branching regulators
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Mediates degradation-dependent SL signaling; indirectly affects ABA-responsive and drought-responsive gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential signaling component required for plant response to SLs
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for SL response; max2 mutants are highly branched and show altered drought/salt responses
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; protein interaction framework; stress-response transcriptome analysis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MAX2 is an essential F-box signaling component required for SL responses in Arabidopsis. 2. It acts in an SCF ubiquitin ligase complex that likely targets repressors for degradation. 3. max2 mutants show branching and abiotic stress phenotypes, indicating a central role in SL-dependent and related signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: D14; DAD2; SCF complex components; D53-related repressors
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: SL biosynthetic genes are under feedback regulation through signaling involving MAX2
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Drought; salt stress; GR24 treatment; karrikin signaling context
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating MAX2 can strongly alter SL sensitivity, branching, and stress responses, but broad pleiotropy limits straightforward deployment
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong pleiotropy due to shared roles in karrikin and other signaling pathways; drought/stress and developmental effects
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not biosynthetic; affects signaling specificity and stress responses. Review notes MAX2 also participates in karrikin signaling and possibly other pathways.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: α/β-fold hydrolase with catalytic Ser-His-Asp triad
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Strigolactone perception and initiation of signaling
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Ligand binding/hydrolysis-dependent receptor activation leading to interaction with MAX2/D3 and repressor degradation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly enables downstream SL-responsive transcriptional and developmental changes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for SL response; binds/hydrolyzes SL analogs and triggers downstream signaling leading to repressor degradation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical ligand binding/hydrolysis assays; crystal structures; protein interaction assays; mutant genetics
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. D14 is the core SL receptor, an α/β-hydrolase that binds and hydrolyzes SL analogs. 2. Ligand-triggered conformational change promotes interaction with MAX2/D3. 3. This signaling culminates in degradation of repressors such as D53 and controls branching responses.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: GR24 and other SL analog treatments
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A target for tuning SL sensitivity rather than SL production; useful for architecture control if pleiotropy can be managed
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changes in perception could affect multiple SL-regulated developmental processes and beneficial symbioses
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Most direct biochemical evidence in the review used synthetic SL analogs rather than natural SLs.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Proteasomal degradation upon D14-dependent SL perception through D3/MAX2 pathway
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Acts as a repressor of SL downstream signaling until degraded
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major downstream repressor whose removal is necessary for SL response
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: D53 degradation is required for normal SL response; mutant identified in a high-tillering rice background
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; degradation assays in response to GR24; genetic interaction with D14 and D3
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. D53 was identified as a repressor of SL signaling in rice. 2. Upon GR24 perception by D14, D53 is degraded through the D3-dependent proteasome pathway. 3. This established a mechanistic link between SL perception and downstream transcriptional/developmental responses.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to alter SL responsiveness and tillering, though likely highly pleiotropic
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong developmental effects through pathway-wide derepression
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core signaling repressor rather than a biosynthetic regulator.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional control of SL biosynthesis under symbiosis/nutrient signaling
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: MtD27; MtMAX1; OsD27 (via OsNSP1 knockdown evidence)
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller linking symbiosis regulators to SL biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for full expression of SL biosynthetic genes; nsp1 mutation drastically reduces MtD27 transcripts and OsNSP1 knockdown reduces D27 expression and increases tiller number
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant and knockdown analyses; transcript measurement
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NSP1 positively regulates expression of key SL biosynthetic genes under nutrient-deficiency/symbiosis-related conditions. 2. MtD27 transcripts are almost undetectable in nsp1 mutants. 3. OsNSP1 knockdown in rice causes strong reduction of D27 expression and increased tillering, linking NSP1 to SL-mediated architecture control.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for coordinating SL production with nutrient acquisition and symbiosis traits
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May affect rhizobial/mycorrhizal symbiosis broadly beyond SL metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review directly highlights its control of core SL biosynthetic genes.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional control of SL biosynthesis under symbiosis/nutrient signaling
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: MtD27; MtMAX1; OsD27 (via OsNSP2 knockdown evidence)
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller linking symbiosis regulators to SL biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports expression of SL biosynthetic genes; nsp2 mutation reduces MtD27 transcripts to about 20% of WT and OsNSP2 knockdown lowers D27 expression with increased tillering
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant and knockdown analyses; transcript measurement
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NSP2 positively regulates D27/MAX1 expression in nutrient-deficiency contexts. 2. MtD27 expression is strongly reduced in nsp2 mutants. 3. OsNSP2 knockdown lowers D27 expression and increases tiller number, supporting a positive role in SL biosynthesis and function.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering nutrient-responsive SL biosynthesis and symbiosis-associated architecture
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad effects on symbiosis pathways likely
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review explicitly assigns it direct control over core SL biosynthetic genes.
